question: how to make compost tea fertilizer
title: How to Make Compost Tea: A Microbial Fertilizer You Brew at Home
metaDescription: Compost tea is a liquid microbial fertilizer made by aerating compost in water for 24 hours. Scotty walks you through the biology and the simple process step by step.
content:
Here's something that changed how I think about fertilizing. Once I stopped worrying about NPK numbers and started thinking about microbial life, compost tea made perfect sense. You take the most biologically active stuff in your garden — good, finished compost — and you brew it into a liquid that multiplies the microbes and delivers them directly to your plants' root zone.

It's not complicated. The supplies are cheap. The results are real. And the whole thing is a direct expression of what good soil biology is supposed to do.

I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book — came across the concept through some of the soil food web literature — and then I just went and tried it. Super easy to make. Here's how.

## What Compost Tea Actually Is

Compost tea is made by soaking finished, high-quality compost in water and bubbling air through it for 24 to 36 hours. The aeration does two things: it oxygenates the water, creating conditions that favor aerobic bacteria and fungi, and it agitates the water enough to dislodge microorganisms from the compost particles and suspend them in the liquid.

The result is a microbially rich solution. Not salt-rich. Not fertilizer-rich in the conventional sense. Microbe-rich. The biology that was living in your compost is now floating in the water, ready to colonize your soil and go to work.

Well-made actively aerated compost tea (AACT) can contain four times as many microbes as the compost it was made from. The aerated brewing environment — oxygen-rich, with a food source added to encourage reproduction — is ideal for aerobic bacteria and beneficial fungi to multiply rapidly.

## What You Need

The setup is minimal:

- A clean five-gallon bucket (use a clean one — contamination from bad microbes can throw the whole brew off)
- Three to four scoops of good finished compost (take out large sticks and leaves)
- Non-chlorinated water — rainwater is ideal, or let tap water sit out 24 hours to off-gas chlorine, or use a dechlorinating filter
- An aquarium air pump with air stone tubing
- Optional: one to two tablespoons of unsulfured molasses to feed the bacteria

Total cost for the setup, if you don't already have an aquarium pump, is maybe fifteen dollars.

## The Brewing Process

Fill your bucket with water. Some folks like to put the compost in a mesh bag or old pillowcase to make straining easier later — I've done both, and the bag makes cleanup easier without noticeably affecting quality.

Drop the compost in the water. If you're adding molasses, add it now — just a tablespoon or two. Molasses feeds the bacteria and helps the population multiply faster during the brew. Don't overdo it. You want to feed the microbes, not overwhelm the system.

Submerge your air stone, connect it to the pump, and turn it on. You want vigorous bubbling throughout the bucket — enough aeration to keep the entire volume of water well-oxygenated.

Let it brew for 24 to 36 hours. You'll notice the water gets darker and may develop a slight earthy smell — that's normal and good. It should smell like healthy earth. Not sour, not foul. If it smells bad, something went wrong with your compost or your brew conditions.

## How to Use It — and When

This part is critical. Use your compost tea within four to six hours of turning off the air pump. Once the aeration stops, the dissolved oxygen drops quickly, and the aerobic microbes you've just multiplied start dying off as conditions turn anaerobic. The tea goes from probiotic to problematic fast. Don't brew it and leave it sitting.

Apply it immediately after straining. You can use it two ways:

**Soil drench:** Pour it around the base of plants and water it in. This delivers the microbial community directly to the root zone, where it can colonize and begin building the soil food web. Use about a quart per established plant, or water your bed thoroughly.

**Foliar spray:** Strain it well through cheesecloth or a fine mesh screen, load it into a spray bottle or garden sprayer, and apply to leaves early in the morning or late in the afternoon. The microbes colonize the leaf surface and help outcompete pathogenic fungi and bacteria that cause plant disease. Spray in the cooler parts of the day so the microbes aren't killed by heat before they can establish.

Compost tea can be applied weekly during the growing season without any risk of over-application. You cannot burn your plants with it. No salts, no concentrated nitrogen. Just biology.

## Why This Matters More Than Synthetic Fertilizer

Synthetic fertilizers give plants nutrients directly — nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium in salt form. The plant takes them up. Done.

Compost tea gives your soil a living community that creates and delivers nutrients continuously, improves soil structure, suppresses disease, and helps plants access minerals they couldn't reach on their own. It's not a one-time delivery — it's restoring a system.

Gabe Brown talks about this in terms of letting biology do the heavy lifting. Synthetic fertilizers bypass the biology. Compost tea amplifies it. If you've been putting synthetic fertilizers on your soil for years, you've likely reduced the native microbial population. Compost tea is one of the fastest ways to start restoring it.

The soil food web — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, earthworms — is what turns raw minerals into plant-available nutrition. Once you understand that, you stop trying to feed the plant and start trying to feed the biology. Compost tea is one of the most direct expressions of that shift.

## Sources

- [The Ultimate Compost Tea Guide — KiS Organics](https://www.kisorganics.com/blogs/news/the-ultimate-compost-tea-guide)
- [How to Make Compost Tea to Fertilize Your Garden — Homestead and Chill](https://homesteadandchill.com/actively-aerated-compost-tea/)
- [Bubbling with Life: How to Brew and Use Aerated Compost Tea — Nurtured Life](https://nurturedlife.com.au/aerated-compost-tea/)
- [Compost Tea Research Guides — New York Botanical Garden Library](https://libguides.nybg.org/compostteas)
- [Compost Tea Recipe — Smiling Gardener](https://www.smilinggardener.com/soil-food-web/compost-tea-recipe/)
